# Questions in Livestream 213

Live Stream #213: Exploring the Sewer World

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=588" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:48</a> | Do Egyptian and Semitic languages point back to a common ancestor now called Proto-Afroasiatic? | Yes. He treats that shared linguistic ancestry as real. | He says he sees nothing problematic in Proto-Afroasiatic as a linguistic classification. Egyptian and Semitic appear related, so a shared root language is plausible, though he frames it as a theory he would abandon if a better explanation came along. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=658" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:58</a> | What does the word ark mean? | He explains it as a container or vessel used to carry or protect something, with different uses in different contexts. | He distinguishes the English word from the Hebrew terms. Noah's ark uses `tebah`, which he explains as a box or chest rather than a word meaning boat, while the ark of the covenant uses `aron`, closer to a chest, coffer, or coffin; the English "ark" carries the contextual sense of a protective vessel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:01</a> | Do the Middle Bronze houses at Jericho support Rahab living in the wall? | He says the archaeological setting can fit the biblical description once the wall system is understood correctly. | He pushes back on identifying houses built against the outside of the outer wall as Rahab's setting. Reading Joshua 2:15, he says the Hebrew stresses that Rahab lived in the wall itself, and houses outside the city wall are therefore not a convincing match. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=967" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:07</a> | Why do some translations say Rahab was living on the wall? | He says translators often flatten the archaeological picture and miss how the wall complex actually worked. | He says translators are usually not archaeologists and may not have casemate walls in mind. In a casemate system, people could remove fill between parallel walls and build living spaces inside them, so he thinks "in the wall" better reflects the residence described in Joshua. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=1055" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:35</a> | How high would the spies' window have been? | He estimates roughly twenty feet if the house was built into the wall complex. | His estimate depends on Rahab's house being inside the wall structure rather than merely near it. In that scenario, a window used to lower the spies by rope would likely be about twenty feet up. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=1154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:14</a> | Will you do a video on Hazor's phases and their implications for conquest models? | He says he might, but it is not something he can promise soon. | He says the topic could go on an ideas list, especially in relation to Middle Bronze II, Late Bronze I, and conquest theories, but he has not given it much thought and already has a robust list of planned topics. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=1209" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:09</a> | What were grooming and shaving practices outside Egypt, and was lice as bad there too? | He says practices varied, but dense populations and limited hygiene could make lice a serious problem across the region. | He contrasts Egypt with other Near Eastern cultures where men commonly wore full beards, including Israelites and Assyrians. He says lice were especially associated with dense living conditions, and Egypt's tightly packed houses and villages made outbreaks easier, though the same principle could apply anywhere people lived closely together. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=1368" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:48</a> | Did they have schools in Egypt? | Yes. He says Egypt did have schools. | The answer is brief, but he affirms that schools existed in Egypt. In the surrounding discussion he also describes crowded, multi-story houses and shared walls, using that setting to explain Egyptian population density rather than expanding on school organization. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=1460" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:20</a> | Did the Nile return to normal after seven days before the frog plague, or did the events overlap longer? | He allows for a short recovery window and does not think the text requires a long normal interval. | Reading Exodus 7:25 into the frog plague, he says the text states that seven days passed after the Nile was struck and then God sent Moses back to Pharaoh. He does not think that wording proves the water returned to normal after exactly seven days; the plague of blood may have lasted seven days or longer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=1666" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:46</a> | What are your thoughts on the Dead Sea Scrolls AI story? | He is skeptical and argues the headline claims outrun what the method actually established. | He says the study used AI on handwriting in relation to scholarly opinion and carbon-14 data, so the result was bounded by its inputs. He treats both carbon dating and AI as useful tools, but warns that neither should be relied on entirely and that the research team overstated what the tool showed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=1914" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:54</a> | What did the AI actually do in that study? | He says it worked within a pre-bounded date range and compared handwriting, so the result was less revolutionary than advertised. | He explains that the AI was given the range created by carbon-14 results and scholarly dating, then asked to compare epigraphy against other Hebrew epigraphic samples. Because the range was already supplied, he expected a middle-range answer rather than an independent discovery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=1973" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:53</a> | Did that study really conclude the Dead Sea Scrolls authors wrote the Bible? | He says that appears to be how the claim was framed, and he treats it as a serious misunderstanding or overstatement. | He says the claim appears to have been a conclusion of the study or its presentation, but he questions whether it was overstatement or ignorance of the Dead Sea Scrolls. He stresses that the scroll writers were copyists, not the authors of the Bible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=2345" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:05</a> | What is your take on the dating of Daniel? | He favors an earlier date and rejects the standard late critical consensus. | He argues that Daniel can reasonably be placed at least in the fifth century BC, partly because he says 1 Enoch is generally dated earlier than the late consensus for Daniel and quotes Daniel. He does not insist on a sixth-century date, but thinks the second-century consensus is not required. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=2409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:09</a> | What do you like about Dune? | He likes how alien it feels while still being coherent and deeply shaped by religion and environment. | He says Dune feels alien but familiar, especially through its desert setting, its imagined future religion, and its warning about cult figures. He praises Dune as Frank Herbert's strongest work because its pieces fit together coherently, unlike some of Herbert's other books that he finds muddy or poorly structured. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=3187" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:07</a> | What do you think about non-institutional churches? | He gives a cautious answer and warns that anti-institutional impulses can drift in unhealthy directions. | He first interprets the question as possibly meaning non-denominational churches and says he has no problem with them in principle. Like any church, he says people should examine the doctrinal statement, theology, and culture to avoid anything strange. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=3248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:08</a> | What issues do you see with The Two Babylons? | He has not read it closely, but says the author's overall track record makes him distrust the book. | Once the book by Alexander Hislop is identified, he says he has not read it and cannot critique its contents directly. Still, he says Hislop's broader work has a poor track record for reliability, so he would not recommend the book. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=3445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:25</a> | Did the Israelites know Yahweh while at Avaris? | Probably not. He says the evidence points instead to Yahweh worship arriving later through Moses and Midian. | He says the evidence from Avaris points to worship of Egyptian gods and some Canaanite deities such as Baal, Resheph, and Astarte, but not Yahweh. His reconstruction is that Moses brought Yahweh worship from Midian and that Israel began worshiping Yahweh during the wilderness period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=3641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:41</a> | After he died, did the writing improve? | In the L. Ron Hubbard aside, he jokes that the posthumous books were actually better written. | In the aside about L. Ron Hubbard and posthumous publishing, he jokes that books released under Hubbard's name after his death were better than those from his lifetime. He frames it as part of a broader joke about authors continuing to publish after death through others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=3846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:06</a> | Why isn't icon veneration efficacious? | He says the practice rests on a flawed view of prayer and mediation. | He argues that icon veneration assumes a thin view of prayer as mainly asking for things, while prayer is more about aligning oneself with God. Since Christ teaches prayer directly to the Father, he says intercession through saints is unnecessary, and he also rejects the council's logic that denying icon power undermines the incarnation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=4089" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:09</a> | On the pro-intercession view, are the saints a bit like telephone operators for our prayers? | He says that analogy is partly fair, but the saints are imagined as interceding and repeating the request, not merely routing it. | He accepts the analogy only partly. On the pro-intercession view, the saints are not merely redirecting a prayer but joining in it, putting in a word on the person's behalf, and seeking a favorable result from God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=4441" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:01</a> | What do I tell people who say Cain and Abel is about faith versus works righteousness? | He says that is not a strong reading of the passage and pushes back against importing that contrast there. | He says the Cain and Abel story is more about grudging sacrifice than faith versus works. Grain offerings were acceptable elsewhere in biblical worship, so the problem was not that Cain offered produce; he reads the issue as Cain's unrighteous, begrudging heart compared with Abel's righteous offering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=4719" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:39</a> | Aside from the Horus bird, what motifs show a bird descending to mark kingship? | He says the broader motif exists but is not as simple as forcing every bird image into one royal template. | He says the motif is not common in Assyrian, Mesopotamian, or Levantine material, but gives an Egyptian example involving Montu. In the Kadesh inscriptions, Montu, a falcon war god distinct from Horus, descends on Ramesses II to give victory, so the closest parallels remain Egyptian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=5131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:31</a> | Have you tried fish sauce? | Yes. He has used it in cooking. | His answer is brief: he has tried fish sauce and used it in cooking. He adds that it worked well in cooking, though he does not use it as a condiment like garum. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=5212" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:52</a> | Why do some people believe Job is the oldest book of the Bible? | He says they usually infer great antiquity from the book's setting and lack of later Israelite markers. | He says the claim often comes from Job's creation themes and older creationist readings that identified chaos monsters with dinosaurs. He rejects that reasoning and points instead to Iron Age features, including iron and the Aramean setting, saying scholars generally place Job much later than the oldest biblical material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=5363" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:23</a> | What do you think is the oldest book of the Bible? | He answers Exodus. | He qualifies that the whole final book may not be the oldest in every respect, but he thinks Exodus preserves the oldest source material. He points to the Song of the Sea, the Ten Commandments, and early route traditions as evidence that Exodus contains very early written sources. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=5709" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:09</a> | Is the Exodus pointing to the cross? | He says the answer depends on perspective: Exodus comes first in sequence, while the cross refers back to Exodus and Passover. | He distinguishes temporal sequence from later theological reference. Exodus comes first and may point forward implicitly, but the New Testament cross explicitly refers back to Exodus and Passover, so the direction depends on the angle from which the question is asked. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=5834" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:14</a> | What do you think of the claim that the partial hieroglyphs on the Grand Slab name ring read Israel? | He rejects it, saying the dating and hieroglyphic reading are linguistically and archaeologically untenable. | He says the inscription is too deeply cut for the proposed date and belongs better in a 19th-dynasty context. He also says the proposed reading does not actually yield Israel and depends on impossible or chronologically wrong hieroglyphic assumptions, which is why he says Egyptologists have dismissed it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=6110" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:50</a> | Which land better fits the phrase land of milk and honey, Egypt or Israel? | He says the phrase fits Israel better rather than the Nile system. | He identifies the milk as goat's milk and the honey as wild honey. Since Egypt by the New Kingdom was more cattle-oriented and wild honey fits the Levant better, he says the phrase describes a pastoral Levantine landscape more naturally than the Nile Valley. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=6416" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:56</a> | How should we understand Ezekiel 26:14 if Tyre still exists? | He distinguishes mainland and island Tyre and argues the prophecy need not mean every later settlement disappears forever. | He argues Ezekiel describes many nations coming against Tyre, not one destruction only. Tyre had a mainland and island component, and he treats the language about becoming bare rock and not being rebuilt as military rhetoric about the kingdom and capital's power being permanently reduced, not a denial that a later port settlement could exist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=6908" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:08</a> | What are the best English words for nakham in Genesis 6:6? | He prefers a rendering closer to sorrow or grief rather than a simplistic idea of divine mistake. | He discusses the verb as involving regret, second thoughts, consolation, or consoling oneself. In Genesis 6:6 he does not take it as God apologizing, but as language of regret or grief over humanity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=7086" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:06</a> | How do critics ignore the large amount of Egyptian influence on Israel? | He says Egyptology is hard and many Old Testament scholars work more from secondary literature than Egyptian primary data. | He says Egyptology is difficult because it requires massive engagement with primary sources such as inscriptions, papyri, ostraca, and stelae. By contrast, he describes Old Testament studies as often consensus- and secondary-literature-driven, so scholars may overlook Egyptian influence because they are not trained to handle the Egyptian evidence directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=7638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:18</a> | Is there another ancient text besides the Bible explained with a documentary hypothesis? | He says no comparable case comes to mind. | He calls the documentary hypothesis a single-purpose theory because, in his view, it is applied uniquely to the Bible. He says scholars do not apply the same framework even to apocryphal texts, philosophy, or other ancient literature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=7752" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:12</a> | Does being a single-purpose theory usually imply that a theory is bad? | Often, yes. He says a good theory should work in general cases as well as specific ones, or it risks becoming ad hoc. | He says a good theory should explain a broad class of cases and then also work in the specific case. If it only works once, he says it becomes too close to an ad hoc explanation, like single-purpose tests that cannot be applied outside the one object they were designed to defend. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=7924" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:04</a> | Will we still be able to study the Bible in heaven? | He sees no reason learning would stop and answers yes in principle. | He says people will not become omniscient in heaven, so questions and learning can continue. Because God's word is eternal, he sees no reason Bible study would cease, though the confusion and clutter that now obscure interpretation would be removed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=8062" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:22</a> | How many important archaeological discoveries have we had for a long time that still need to be rediscovered or reassessed? | He says a surprising number of finds still deserve fresh attention because publication, context, and access lag behind discovery. | He says there is always room to re-examine old finds because new theories, better analysis, and advances in linguistics can change their significance. As an example, he says later Egyptological understanding of determinatives sharpened interpretation of the Merneptah Stele's reference to Israel as a people group rather than a settled city-state. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=8339" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:59</a> | What is the majority religious affiliation of biblical scholars in America and Europe? | He says the field is mixed, with many scholars tied to Christian traditions but wide variation across institutions. | He says Protestants probably remain the largest category if counted by institutional affiliation, but that category includes a wide range from orthodox believers to progressives and atheists. He thinks secular or atheist scholars form the next largest category and says the field is shifting as academic posts decline. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=8459" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:59</a> | How can someone be Protestant and an atheist; did you misspeak? | He says he did not misspeak and points to scholars who remained institutionally Protestant while functionally unbelieving. | He says someone can remain affiliated with a Protestant institution while privately disbelieving, citing Rudolf Bultmann and other examples. His point is that institutional or professional affiliation does not always match personal belief. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=8746" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:46</a> | What is your response to the Daniel-dating argument based on Antiochus IV supposedly being predicted wrongly? | He says the argument is overstated and does not force a late date for Daniel. | He argues Daniel 11 is not exclusively about Antiochus IV. Antiochus functions as an archetype, but parts of the passage move to an antitype and end-time horizon, so he rejects the claim that apparent mismatches with Antiochus prove Daniel was written late and guessed wrong. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=9290" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:50</a> | Was Joseph mummified when he died? | Yes. He reads Genesis 50:26 that way. | He says mummification was the standard burial practice for Egypt's upper and middle classes. Since Jacob was mummified and Joseph held the status of vizier, Joseph would have received the kind of elite Egyptian burial treatment expected for someone of his class. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=9370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:10</a> | What is the Egyptian context to Joseph's divination cup in Genesis 44:5? | He says the episode fits Egyptian prestige objects and court symbolism rather than random fantasy detail. | He emphasizes that Joseph's cup is described as silver, a very high-value object, and compares it to Egyptian divination cups used by pouring water or wine into them and reading images. He shows a large alabaster cup from Tutankhamun's tomb as an example of the kind of object used for divination, while noting Joseph's was silver. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=9987" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:27</a> | What do you think of Inanna-and-Jesus parallels? | He rejects the comparison as shallow parallel-hunting. | He says alleged parallels require propinquity, meaning a real geographical or temporal connection, and he does not see that between Inanna and Jesus. He also distinguishes agricultural-cycle dying-and-rising myths from Jesus defeating death and sin once for all. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=10160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:20</a> | Would a first-century Galilean even know who Inanna is? | No. He says they would not. | He answers directly that a first-century Galilean would not know Inanna. In the surrounding discussion he uses that lack of cultural connection as a reason the claimed borrowing parallels fail. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=10407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:27</a> | Do you have experience with Baptists? | Yes. He says he does, and describes them as often fun but sometimes quite legalistic. | He says Baptists can be fun but also restrictive, especially about alcohol, dancing, gambling, and similar practices. He says he has interacted with Southern Baptists, though he has never been one himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=11096" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:56</a> | What is your chronology for the writing of the Pentateuch? | He places Exodus first, then Numbers, then Genesis, Leviticus, and Deuteronomy near the end of the wilderness period, with Joshua or a scribe later arranging them into five books. | He says most source documents were written during the Sinai wandering. In his reconstruction, Exodus came first, then Numbers, then Genesis, Leviticus, and Deuteronomy near the end, with Joshua or a scribe later arranging the material into five books with connective text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=11168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:08</a> | Are there corresponding names to other kings in Genesis 14 besides Amraphel? | He says yes, especially for Chedorlaomer-type identifications, though there are too many possible Elamite matches for certainty. | He says Chedorlaomer is the other major name with possible historical correspondences, but the problem is that the Elamite name is attested too often. There are multiple possible Elamite kings in Hammurabi's period, so the evidence is suggestive but not precise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=11265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:45</a> | Why do scholars reject the Amraphel-Hammurabi identification? | He says secular scholars reject it because it would support biblical historicity, while very conservative scholars reject it because Hammurabi is too late for their early Abraham chronology. | He says both ends reject the identification for different presuppositional reasons. Secular scholars resist a historical link to Genesis 14, while very conservative scholars dislike the chronological implication that Abraham belongs in the 18th century rather than an earlier Middle Kingdom framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=11484" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:24</a> | Why are the vowels different in Hammurabi and Amraphel? | He says the vowel differences are not decisive in Semitic languages; the consonants matter more, and the main issue is explaining the final consonant in the Hebrew form. | He explains Hammurabi as a Mesopotamian form of an Amorite name related to Amarapi, with consonantal correspondences doing the main work. Vowels shift readily in Semitic languages, while the final `l` in Amraphel is the more debated point, which he relates to Hebrew's dislike of final vowels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=11738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:38</a> | Are you saying Abraham dates to Hammurabi? | Yes. He says Abraham does date to Hammurabi, though many scholars disagree. | He clarifies that he does think Abraham dates to Hammurabi and considers that a solid dating. He acknowledges that many scholars disagree, but he treats the Hammurabi connection as historically plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=12260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:20</a> | Where on that spectrum did Kenneth Kitchen fall? | He says Kitchen leaned strongly toward rigor. | In a discussion of rigorous versus creative scholars, he says Kitchen and most Liverpool-trained scholars were strongly on the rigor side. He contrasts Kitchen with Mark Collier, whom he describes as still rigorous but somewhat more balanced toward creativity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=12429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:09</a> | Would writing the vowels be especially hard when making Hebrew manuscript reconstructions? | He says vowels require caution because they come through later Masoretic pointing, and even word spacing can reflect later interpretive decisions. | He says vowels were not part of the original Hebrew manuscript tradition and come through the Masoretes' later pointing, so they should be handled carefully and are not inspired in the same way as the consonantal text. He also notes that even word spacing was added later and can sometimes reflect a wrong interpretive decision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=12676" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:16</a> | Where do you fall on the rigor-versus-creativity spectrum? | He puts himself somewhat on the creative side while still valuing rigor. | He says he is just over the line into the creative side, but strongly believes creativity must be tempered by rigor. Without that discipline, he says creative scholarship can lead to strange and unsupported conclusions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=12774" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:54</a> | Why didn't the Egyptians do better at preserving papyrus? | He says most preservation was accidental and depended on desert conditions, not deliberate archival success. | He says surviving papyrus mostly comes from Upper Egypt because desert conditions preserved it. Lower Egypt's high, salty water table destroys papyrus and even stone, which is why sites such as Avaris have yielded seals and tiny tablet fragments but very few readable texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=12954" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:54</a> | Did the Egyptians have any hard liquor? | No. He says wine was as strong as their ordinary alcohol got. | He says Egyptians got drunk on wine, probably around 12 to 13 percent alcohol, rather than distilled liquor. If they wanted a stronger altered state, he says they could combine wine with lotus juice or lotus petals, producing a different psychedelic experience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=13051" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:31</a> | Can you give examples of times Kip Davis was wrong? | He names Jericho claims as clear examples and says those arguments were plainly mistaken. | He says Kip Davis was wrong to claim there was no Late Bronze Age Jericho, wrong about early Bronze Age casemate walls at Jericho, and wrong about identifying Amurru words as Amorites. He presents these as examples outside Davis's wheelhouse where the evidence does not support him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=13244" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:44</a> | Do you think the Garden of Eden was in southern Iraq? | Yes, probably in or near the southern Mesopotamian delta region. | He says the Tigris and Euphrates are known and places the likely setting in the southern Mesopotamian floodplain or delta, especially the Sealand region. Because the coastline has shifted, he thinks the relevant area may no longer exist in the same form today. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=13384" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:04</a> | What does Ken Ham get right? | He says Ham gets some foundational things right, such as the importance of Genesis and the inspiration of Scripture. | He says it is hard for anyone to be entirely wrong, and grants that Ken Ham is right that Genesis is important, Scripture is inspired, and much of the Bible contains historical information. He also says Answers in Genesis has shown some movement toward better rigor, even though he still has serious disagreements. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C8LR4z1tFnQ?t=13994" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:14</a> | Does Job take place outside Israel? | Yes. He says the setting is outside Israel. | He places Job in Aram, in the Syrian region outside Israel proper. He describes it as a period after the fall of Mitanni and before the Aramean states fully coalesced, a disorderly Iron Age setting with raiders and weak government. |
